summary: Jeff Atwood explores what an ideal login experience should look like by applying the 'God algorithm' concept he learned from Randy Pausch: asking what the perfect, instantaneous solution would be, then working backward to get as close as possible. Since God wouldn't need a login dialog at all (users would already be authenticated), the goal becomes minimizing friction in the inevitable compromise. The post systematically examines every aspect of login UX: using email as identity, revealing whether emails exist in the system, seamlessly toggling between login and signup, supporting password managers, handling common mistakes like caps lock and email typos, enforcing minimum password quality, and rate limiting to prevent abuse. Atwood concludes that while a two-field form seems simple, it's deceptively complex, and the best approach is to avoid building login dialogs altogether when possible.
key_points: The 'God algorithm' concept asks what the perfect O(1) solution would be, then works backward to approximate it
Email should always be accepted as login identity since it's already required for password recovery
Telling users when their email doesn't exist in the forgot password form is more user-friendly than the security-conscious alternative of being coy about it
Login and signup are so similar from users' perspectives that switching between them should be seamless
A login form must work with browser password managers, handle common user mistakes like caps lock and email typos, and rate limit attempts to prevent abuse
money_quotes: What's the God algorithm for this?
God wouldn't bother with a stupid Bubble Sort or Quick Sort or Shell Sort like us mere mortals, God would just immediately place the items in the correct order. Bam. One step.
How would God build this login dialog? And the answer is, of course, God wouldn't bother to build a login dialog at all.
This is obviously impossible for us, because God isn't one of our investors.
User identity is always email, plain and simple. What happens when you forget your password? You get an email, right? Thus, email is your identity.
Being user friendly is way more important than being secure.
Logging in involves a simple form with two fields, a link, and two buttons. And yet, after reading all this, I'm sure you'll agree that it's deceptively complex.
key_insight: The best login experience comes from asking what the ideal frictionless solution would look like, then systematically removing every possible point of user confusion and frustration to get as close to that ideal as reality allows.
